event: Early Stone Age (Acheulean) tool-making humans inhabit the region of present-day Ghana, leaving some of West Africa's earliest evidence of human presence.
Late Stone Age hunter-gatherer communities occupy rock shelters and forest fringes across the area, as seen at sites like Bosumpra Cave near Abetifi.
Neolithic communities begin cultivating crops and making pottery, marking the transition to settled farming life in the forest and savanna zones.
Iron-smelting technology spreads into the region, enabling more advanced tools, agriculture, and population growth.
Akan-speaking peoples establish settled agricultural communities, laying the foundations for later forest states based on gold and trade.
Trans-Saharan trade routes link the goldfields of the forest zone to North Africa, drawing the region into wider commercial networks.
Portuguese navigators reach the coast and, finding abundant gold, name the area the Gold Coast (Costa do Ouro).
The Portuguese build Elmina Castle (São Jorge da Mina), the first permanent European fortification in sub-Saharan Africa, to control the gold trade.
The Dutch begin establishing trading posts on the coast, challenging Portuguese dominance over Gold Coast commerce.
The Dutch capture Elmina Castle from the Portuguese, becoming the leading European power on the coast.
European demand shifts increasingly toward enslaved people, and Gold Coast forts become major embarkation points in the transatlantic slave trade.
Osei Tutu unites Akan states into the powerful Asante Empire, centered at Kumasi and symbolized by the Golden Stool.
Britain abolishes the slave trade, gradually transforming the economy of its Gold Coast settlements.
The First Anglo-Asante War sees British forces defeated and Governor Sir Charles MacCarthy killed in battle against the Asante.
Coastal Fante chiefs sign the Bond of 1844 with Britain, formalizing British legal influence over parts of the Gold Coast.
Britain purchases the Danish forts on the Gold Coast, expanding its territorial control.
Britain acquires the Dutch possessions, including Elmina, becoming the sole European power on the coast.
Britain formally establishes the Gold Coast Colony after sacking Kumasi in the Third Anglo-Asante War.
British forces occupy Kumasi and exile the Asantehene Prempeh I, bringing Asante under colonial control.
The War of the Golden Stool erupts when Yaa Asantewaa leads Asante resistance against British demands for the sacred stool.
Britain formally annexes Asante and the Northern Territories, completing colonial control over the Gold Coast.
Police fire on protesting ex-servicemen in Accra, sparking nationwide riots that galvanize the independence movement.
Kwame Nkrumah founds the Convention People's Party (CPP), demanding 'self-government now.'
The CPP wins the first general election under a new constitution, and Nkrumah becomes Leader of Government Business.
Ghana becomes the first sub-Saharan African colony to gain independence from Britain, with Kwame Nkrumah as prime minister, and joins the United Nations.
Ghana becomes a republic with Kwame Nkrumah as its first president.
Ghana becomes a founding member of the Organisation of African Unity, reflecting Nkrumah's pan-African vision.
A military coup overthrows Nkrumah while he is abroad, ending his one-party rule and installing the National Liberation Council.
Civilian rule returns under Prime Minister Kofi Busia after national elections.
Colonel Ignatius Kutu Acheampong seizes power in a coup, ushering in years of military government and economic decline.
Flight Lieutenant Jerry Rawlings leads a coup and oversees a brief period before handing power to an elected civilian government.
Rawlings seizes power again in a second coup, establishing the Provisional National Defence Council and ruling for over a decade.
Ghana adopts an IMF-backed Economic Recovery Programme amid drought, famine, and the return of nearly a million Ghanaians expelled from Nigeria.
A new constitution restores multiparty democracy, and Jerry Rawlings wins the presidential election under the Fourth Republic.
John Kufuor wins the presidency, marking Ghana's first peaceful democratic transfer of power between parties.
Large offshore oil reserves are discovered in the Jubilee Field, transforming Ghana's economic prospects.
John Atta Mills is inaugurated president after another peaceful electoral transition, and U.S. President Obama visits Accra.
Ghana begins commercial oil production and the Black Stars reach the FIFA World Cup quarter-finals, a record for an African team at the time.
President John Atta Mills dies in office and is succeeded by Vice President John Dramani Mahama, who then wins the December election.
Nana Akufo-Addo is inaugurated president after defeating the incumbent, continuing Ghana's tradition of peaceful power transfers.
Ghana launches the 'Year of Return,' inviting the African diaspora to commemorate 400 years since the first enslaved Africans arrived in Virginia.
Ghana faces a severe debt and currency crisis, seeking a multibillion-dollar IMF bailout amid soaring inflation.
John Dramani Mahama returns to the presidency after winning the December 2024 election, completing another peaceful democratic transition.
